lessons.title: Momentum: Mass Times Velocity
lessons.content:
Imagine trying to stop a slow-rolling shopping cart with your hand. Easy. Now imagine stopping a truck rolling at the same slow speed. No chance. Both are moving, yet one is far "harder to stop" than the other. Physics captures this idea with a quantity called **momentum**.

**Build:** Momentum is a measure of how much *motion* an object carries, and it depends on two things: how heavy it is and how fast it goes. Its formula is mass times velocity:

$$p = m \\times v$$

Here $p$ is the momentum, $m$ is the **mass** (how much matter is in the object, measured in kilograms, kg), and $v$ is the **velocity** (its speed in a particular direction, measured in meters per second, m/s). Multiply them and momentum comes out in units of **kilogram-meters per second** ($\\text{kg} \\cdot \\text{m/s}$).

Because both mass and velocity count, a heavy slow truck and a light fast car can carry the *same* momentum. A 4000 kg truck at 2 m/s has $p = 8000\\;\\text{kg}\\cdot\\text{m/s}$, and so does a 1000 kg car at 8 m/s. Momentum also has a direction (the way the object moves), so it is a quantity with both size and direction.

**Recap:** Momentum $p = m \\times v$ tells you how hard something is to stop. More mass or more speed means more momentum, measured in $\\text{kg}\\cdot\\text{m/s}$.

items.prompt: What is the formula for momentum?
choices: p = m x v (mass times velocity)
p = m / v (mass divided by velocity)
p = v / m (velocity divided by mass)
items.explanation: Momentum is mass multiplied by velocity: p = m x v. It depends on both factors together, so you multiply them; adding or dividing would not capture how a heavier OR faster object carries more motion.

items.prompt: What is the unit of momentum?
items.explanation: Since p = mass (kg) times velocity (m/s), the unit is kg*m/s. The joule is energy, the newton is force, and m/s alone is just velocity without the mass factor.

items.prompt: A 2 kg ball moves at 3 m/s. What is its momentum?
items.explanation: p = m x v = 2 x 3 = 6 kg*m/s. The answer 5 comes from adding (2+3), and 1.5 from dividing (3/2) - neither matches the multiply rule.

items.prompt: Why is a moving truck harder to stop than a shopping cart at the same speed?
choices: The truck has much more mass, so much more momentum
The truck has more velocity than the cart
Speed alone decides how hard something is to stop
items.explanation: At the same speed, the heavier truck has far more mass and therefore far more momentum (p = m x v). Velocity is equal here, so the difference comes entirely from mass; speed alone does not decide it.

items.prompt: A 4000 kg truck moves at 2 m/s. A 1000 kg car moves at 8 m/s. How do their momenta compare?
choices: The truck has more because it is heavier
The car has more because it is faster
You cannot compare them without knowing the distance
items.explanation: Truck: 4000 x 2 = 8000 kg*m/s. Car: 1000 x 8 = 8000 kg*m/s. They match. A heavy-slow object can carry the same momentum as a light-fast one; mass and speed trade off.

items.prompt: If you double an object's velocity but keep its mass the same, what happens to its momentum?
items.explanation: Momentum is directly proportional to velocity (p = m x v), so doubling v doubles p. It quadruples only if you were squaring v - but momentum uses v to the first power, unlike kinetic energy.

items.prompt: What two properties of an object determine its momentum?
choices: Its weight and the distance it travels
items.explanation: Momentum is mass times velocity, so only those two matter. Color, temperature, and distance traveled do not appear in p = m x v.

items.prompt: Besides a size (amount), what else does momentum have?
choices: A direction (the way the object is moving)
Nothing - it is just a single number with no direction
items.explanation: Momentum points in the direction of motion, so it has both size and direction (it is a vector). This matters later: momenta in opposite directions can partly cancel.

items.prompt: An object is sitting completely still. What is its momentum?
choices: Zero, because its velocity is zero
Very large, because it is heavy
items.explanation: p = m x v, and a still object has v = 0, so p = 0 no matter how heavy it is. Mass alone is not momentum; you need motion too.

lessons.title: Impulse: Force Over Time Changes Momentum
lessons.content:
Why does landing on a soft mattress feel fine, but landing on concrete from the same height can break a bone? In both cases your body loses the same amount of momentum - you go from moving to stopped. The difference is how the stop happens. This is the idea of **impulse**.

**Build:** A **force** is a push or pull, measured in **newtons (N)**. When a force acts for a stretch of **time**, it delivers an **impulse**, which equals force multiplied by the time it acts:

$$\\text{impulse} = F \\times t$$

The key law is that impulse equals the **change in momentum** (written $\\Delta p$, where the symbol $\\Delta$ means "change in"):

$$F \\times t = \\Delta p$$

Now rearrange the thinking. To stop a moving object, you must remove a fixed amount of momentum, $\\Delta p$. That can be done with a big force over a short time, or a small force over a long time - the product is the same. **Stretching out the stopping time shrinks the force.** That is exactly what an airbag, a crumple zone, bending your knees on landing, or a boxer "rolling with" a punch all do: they lengthen the time so the force stays gentle. Follow-through in sport is the same idea in reverse - keep the force on longer to add more momentum.

**Recap:** Impulse $F \\times t = \\Delta p$. The same change in momentum can come from a hard quick hit or a gentle long one; longer time means smaller force.

items.prompt: What does impulse equal?
choices: Force multiplied by the time it acts
items.explanation: Impulse = F x t. Dividing force by time gives a rate, not impulse. Impulse is how much 'push over time' is delivered, which changes an object's momentum.

items.prompt: Impulse is equal to what other quantity?
items.explanation: The impulse-momentum law says F x t equals the change in momentum (delta p). It is about momentum, not energy, weight, or distance.

items.prompt: How does an airbag protect you in a crash?
choices: It lengthens the stopping time, so the force on you is smaller
It reduces how much momentum you have to lose
It removes your momentum before the crash
It makes the car heavier so it stops faster
items.explanation: A common misconception is that airbags 'reduce the momentum change.' They do not - you still go from moving to stopped, the same delta p. They stretch the stopping time, so the same impulse is delivered with a much smaller (safer) force.

items.prompt: You must stop a moving cart, removing a fixed amount of momentum. If you take a LONGER time to stop it, the force needed is:
items.explanation: Since F x t equals a fixed delta p, increasing t means F must decrease. Spreading the stop over more time always lowers the force - the principle behind soft landings and crumple zones.

items.prompt: Why do you bend your knees when landing from a jump?
choices: To extend the stopping time and reduce the force on your legs
To stop instantly with no force
To make yourself heavier on landing
items.explanation: Bending your knees lengthens the time over which you slow to a stop. The same momentum change spread over more time means a gentler force. Landing stiff-legged stops you fast - a large, jarring force.

items.prompt: Two stops remove the same change in momentum. One uses a big force for a short time; the other a small force for a long time. On a force-vs-time graph, these two stops have:
choices: The same area (a tall-thin region and a short-wide region)
Different areas, the hard hit being larger
Different areas, the gentle stop being larger
items.explanation: On a force-vs-time graph the area equals the impulse (F x t). Equal momentum change means equal area: a tall-narrow box (hard hit) and a short-wide box (gentle stop) cover the same area.

items.prompt: What does the symbol the Greek letter delta mean in 'delta p'?
choices: The 'change in' a quantity (here, change in momentum)
items.explanation: Delta means 'change in.' So delta p is the change in momentum (final minus initial). It is not a total, a rate, or a square.

items.prompt: Landing on concrete hurts more than landing on a mattress from the same height because:
choices: Concrete stops you in a much shorter time, so the force is much larger
You have more momentum when landing on concrete
The mattress removes your momentum before you land
Concrete reduces the change in momentum
items.explanation: The momentum change is the same either way (you stop from the same speed). Concrete brings you to rest almost instantly - tiny time, huge force. The mattress stretches the time, shrinking the force.

items.prompt: Why does 'following through' on a tennis swing send the ball faster?
choices: Keeping the racket pushing the ball longer increases the impulse, adding more momentum
It only looks faster but changes nothing
items.explanation: Follow-through keeps the force acting for more time, so F x t (impulse) is larger, which means a larger momentum change - the ball leaves faster. It is the same impulse idea used to ADD momentum instead of remove it.

lessons.title: Conservation of Momentum
lessons.content:
Fire a rifle and it kicks back into your shoulder. Step off a small boat and the boat slides backward. A rocket pushes gas out the back and lurches forward. These all obey one of the most powerful rules in physics: **conservation of momentum**.

**Build:** A **closed system** means a group of objects with no outside push or pull acting on them (no friction, no outside force). The rule is simple: in a closed system, the **total momentum stays the same** - the total before any interaction equals the total after:

$$p_{\\text{before}} = p_{\\text{after}}$$

Objects can shove each other and swap momentum around, but the *sum* never changes. Remember momentum has direction, so a forward momentum of $+6$ and a backward momentum of $-6$ add up to zero.

This explains **recoil**. Before firing, a rifle and bullet are at rest: total momentum zero. After firing, the bullet flies forward with some momentum, so the rifle must move backward with exactly equal-and-opposite momentum to keep the total at zero. A **rocket** works the same way: it throws gas backward, so the rocket gains equal forward momentum. Nothing needs to be 'pushed against' - the gas going one way and the rocket the other always balance.

**Recap:** In a closed system the total momentum before equals the total after. Recoil and rockets work because momenta gained in opposite directions cancel, keeping the total fixed.

items.prompt: What does conservation of momentum state?
choices: In a closed system, the total momentum before equals the total after
Each object keeps its own momentum unchanged
items.explanation: The total momentum of a closed system is conserved: before = after. Individual objects can change momentum, but the SUM is fixed; it does not grow, vanish, or stay the same for each object separately.

items.prompt: What is a 'closed system' in this context?
choices: A set of objects with no outside force acting on them
A system kept in a sealed box
A system that is not moving
items.explanation: A closed system means no external forces (like friction or an outside push). That is the condition under which total momentum is conserved. It is not about being in a box or being at rest.

items.prompt: A rifle fires a bullet forward. Why does the rifle kick backward?
choices: The total momentum was zero, so the backward rifle balances the forward bullet
The bullet pushes the air, which pushes the rifle
The rifle gains momentum from nowhere
items.explanation: Before firing, total momentum is zero. The bullet leaves with forward momentum, so the rifle must take equal backward momentum to keep the total at zero. Conservation is obeyed, not broken.

items.prompt: How does a rocket move forward in empty space, with nothing to push against?
choices: It throws gas backward, gaining equal forward momentum
It pushes against the air outside
It cannot move in empty space
items.explanation: A rocket ejects gas backward; conservation of momentum means it gains equal-and-opposite forward momentum. It needs nothing external to push on - which is why rockets work in the vacuum of space.

items.prompt: Cart A (p = +6) hits stationary cart B (p = 0). After the collision A has p = +2. What is B's momentum?
items.explanation: Total before = +6 + 0 = +6. Total after must also be +6, so B's momentum = +6 - 2 = +4. A slowed down and handed the rest of its momentum to B.

items.prompt: Two ice skaters stand still, then push off each other. What is the total momentum just after they push?
items.explanation: They start with zero total momentum. After pushing, one glides one way and the other the opposite way; the equal-and-opposite momenta sum to zero, conserving the total.

items.prompt: Why does direction matter when adding up momentum?
choices: Momentum is a vector, so opposite directions can cancel (e.g. +6 and -6 give 0)
Larger numbers always win regardless of direction
Direction only matters for the heavier object
items.explanation: Momentum has direction, so a forward +6 and a backward -6 add to zero. Ignoring direction would wrongly give 12. This is exactly why recoil keeps a starting total of zero.

items.prompt: If a strong outside force (like a big push from someone outside) acts on a system, is its total momentum still conserved?
choices: No - an outside force can change the total momentum
Yes - momentum is always conserved no matter what
Only if the objects are heavy
items.explanation: Conservation of momentum holds only for a CLOSED system (no outside forces). An external push delivers an impulse that changes the total. Inside a closed system, though, the total stays fixed.

items.prompt: You stand on a stationary skateboard and throw a heavy ball forward. What happens to you?
choices: You roll forward with the ball
items.explanation: Total momentum starts at zero. The ball goes forward with some momentum, so you (plus board) must take equal backward momentum to keep the total zero - you roll back. This is recoil again.

lessons.title: Elastic vs Inelastic Collisions
lessons.content:
When two billiard balls click off each other they bounce apart cleanly. When two lumps of wet clay collide they squish together and stop as one. Both are **collisions** (objects hitting each other), but they behave very differently. The difference is about energy.

**Build:** First, a crucial fact: **momentum is conserved in every collision** of a closed system - elastic or not. What changes is the **kinetic energy (KE)**, the energy an object has because it is moving.

An **elastic collision** is one where the total kinetic energy is *also* conserved: no motion-energy is lost. The objects bounce apart with the same total KE they started with. Hard balls and idealized atoms come close to this.

An **inelastic collision** is one where kinetic energy is *not* conserved - some is turned into heat, sound, or the energy of bending and denting. The clearest case is a **perfectly inelastic collision**, where the objects stick together and move off as one lump (think clay, or train cars coupling). Crashed cars crumpling, a dart hitting a board, a meteor in mud - all inelastic.

The big trap: people assume that if KE is lost, momentum must be lost too. It is not. Even when two cars crumple and stick, their combined momentum after equals their total momentum before. Momentum and kinetic energy are different things, and a collision can keep one while losing the other.

**Recap:** Momentum is conserved in all collisions. Elastic collisions also conserve kinetic energy (bounce apart); inelastic ones lose KE to heat/sound/deformation (often sticking together).

items.prompt: In an elastic collision, what is conserved?
items.explanation: An elastic collision conserves both total momentum AND total kinetic energy. Momentum is conserved in every collision; the special feature of elastic ones is that KE is preserved too.

items.prompt: In an inelastic collision, what happens to kinetic energy?
choices: Some is lost, converting to heat, sound, or deformation
items.explanation: Inelastic collisions lose kinetic energy to heat, sound, and the work of bending/denting. KE is not conserved; it does not increase, and it does not 'become momentum' (different quantities).

items.prompt: Two lumps of clay collide and stick together. Is momentum conserved?
choices: Yes - momentum is conserved even though kinetic energy is not
Only the heavier lump keeps its momentum
Momentum is conserved only in elastic collisions
items.explanation: This is the key misconception. Sticking together is perfectly inelastic, so KE is lost - but momentum is STILL conserved. The combined lump's momentum equals the total before the crash.

items.prompt: What is a 'perfectly inelastic' collision?
choices: One where the objects stick together and move as one
One where the objects bounce apart with no energy lost
One where the objects pass through each other
items.explanation: In a perfectly inelastic collision the objects stick and move off together as a single mass (like coupling train cars). It loses the most KE, but momentum is still conserved.

items.prompt: What is kinetic energy?
choices: The energy an object has because it is moving
items.explanation: Kinetic energy (KE) is energy of motion. It is a separate quantity from momentum - which is why a collision can conserve momentum while losing KE.

items.prompt: Which of these is closest to an elastic collision?
choices: Two hard billiard balls clicking apart
Two cars crumpling and locking together
A dart sticking into a board
A meteor burying itself in mud
items.explanation: Hard billiard balls bounce apart losing very little KE, so they are nearly elastic. The other three lose lots of KE to deformation and sticking - they are inelastic.

items.prompt: True statement about momentum and kinetic energy in collisions:
choices: Momentum is conserved in all collisions, but kinetic energy only in elastic ones
Both are always conserved in every collision
Kinetic energy is always conserved, momentum is not
items.explanation: Momentum is conserved in any closed-system collision. Kinetic energy is conserved only in elastic collisions; in inelastic ones it is partly lost. They are not the same and do not behave the same way.

items.prompt: When two cars crash and crumple, where does the 'lost' kinetic energy go?
choices: Into heat, sound, and bending/denting the metal
It is destroyed and gone from the universe
It stays as kinetic energy in the wreck
items.explanation: Energy is never destroyed - the lost KE becomes heat, sound, and the energy of permanently deforming the metal. It does not become momentum, and it does not stay as motion energy.

items.prompt: A 1 kg ball moving at 4 m/s hits a 1 kg ball at rest and they stick together. What is their common speed afterward?
items.explanation: Momentum before = 1 x 4 = 4 kg*m/s. After, total mass is 2 kg moving at speed v, so 2 x v = 4, giving v = 2 m/s. (Note KE dropped from 8 J to 4 J - lost to the collision - even though momentum was conserved.)
